questions.text: A 50-year-old man presents with sudden severe epigastric pain radiating to the back. He has a history of alcohol abuse. Which retroperitoneal organ lies directly posterior to the stomach and is commonly inflamed in pancreatitis?
questions.explanation: The pancreas is a retroperitoneal organ lying posterior to the stomach. Acute pancreatitis causes epigastric pain radiating to the back due to its retroperitoneal location.

questions.text: A 30-year-old woman with a stab wound to the right anterior chest at the 4th intercostal space, sternal border, develops hypotension and muffled heart sounds. Which cardiac chamber is most likely injured?
questions.explanation: The right ventricle forms most of the anterior cardiac surface and lies behind the 4th–5th intercostal spaces near the sternum. Injury can cause cardiac tamponade (Beck’s triad).

questions.text: A 65-year-old man with a history of smoking presents with hoarseness and a left lung mass. Which nerve is compressed by the tumor at the aortic arch?
questions.explanation: The left recurrent laryngeal nerve loops under the aortic arch. A left lung tumor (especially apical) can compress this nerve, causing hoarseness due to vocal cord paralysis.

questions.text: A patient with chronic liver disease develops massive ascites. Paracentesis is planned. Which abdominal wall landmark is safest to avoid the inferior epigastric artery?
options: Left lower quadrant lateral to rectus sheath
questions.explanation: The inferior epigastric artery runs lateral to the rectus abdominis. Midline puncture (linea alba) is avascular and safest for paracentesis to avoid vessel injury.

questions.text: A 25-year-old man with a fractured humeral shaft cannot extend his wrist or fingers. Which nerve is damaged?
questions.explanation: The radial nerve runs in the spiral groove of the humerus. Fracture of the humeral shaft often injures it, causing wrist drop (loss of wrist and finger extensors).

questions.text: A 40-year-old woman with a thyroid nodule undergoes fine-needle aspiration. The needle passes through the platysma. What is the next fascial layer encountered?
options: Investing layer of deep cervical fascia
questions.explanation: After skin and platysma (in superficial fascia), the next layer is the investing layer of deep cervical fascia, which encloses the sternocleidomastoid and trapezius muscles.

questions.text: A patient with a large right-sided pleural effusion undergoes thoracentesis at the 9th intercostal space, posterior axillary line. Insertion too low may puncture which organ?
questions.explanation: The right hemidiaphragm dome is at the 5th ICS anteriorly, but posteriorly the 9th ICS is above the liver. However, a needle inserted below the 9th ICS risks puncturing the liver.

questions.text: A 60-year-old man with benign prostatic hyperplasia has acute urinary retention. A suprapubic catheter is placed. Which structure lies immediately posterior to the bladder?
questions.explanation: The rectum lies directly posterior to the bladder, separated only by Denonvilliers’ fascia. Suprapubic catheter placement, if too deep, can perforate the rectum.

questions.text: A 35-year-old man with a stab wound to the left upper quadrant has a splenic injury. Which artery is the main blood supply to the spleen and is at risk during splenectomy?
questions.explanation: The splenic artery (branch of celiac trunk) is the main arterial supply to the spleen. It runs along the superior border of the pancreas and must be ligated during splenectomy.

questions.text: A 55-year-old woman with a central venous catheter in the right internal jugular vein develops a pneumothorax. Which structure is most likely punctured?
questions.explanation: The lung apex (cupula) extends above the first rib into the root of the neck, posterior to the internal jugular vein. Deep needle insertion can puncture the pleura, causing pneumothorax.

questions.text: A patient with a deep cut to the medial aspect of the mid-thigh cannot adduct the thigh. Which nerve is injured?
questions.explanation: The obturator nerve (L2–L4) supplies the adductor muscles of the thigh. Injury from a medial thigh wound causes loss of hip adduction.

questions.text: A 70-year-old man with a left-sided inguinal hernia undergoes repair. The hernia sac is lateral to the inferior epigastric vessels. This is an indirect hernia. Which structure does the sac follow?
questions.explanation: Indirect inguinal hernias pass through the deep inguinal ring (lateral to inferior epigastric vessels) and follow the spermatic cord into the scrotum.

questions.text: A 45-year-old man with a knife wound to the anterior neck at the cricoid cartilage level presents with stridor and hoarseness. Which structure is transected?
questions.explanation: The recurrent laryngeal nerve runs in the tracheoesophageal groove. A wound at cricoid level (C6) can damage the nerve, causing ipsilateral vocal cord paralysis and stridor.

questions.text: A patient with a large goiter presents with dysphagia. Which structure is compressed by the enlarged thyroid?
questions.explanation: The esophagus lies posterior to the trachea and left lobe of the thyroid. A large goiter can compress the esophagus, causing dysphagia (difficulty swallowing).

questions.text: A 30-year-old woman with a stab wound to the right 8th intercostal space, midaxillary line, has a right-sided hemothorax. Which vessel is most likely injured?
questions.explanation: The 8th intercostal artery runs in the subcostal groove of the 8th rib. A stab at the 8th ICS will injure this artery, causing bleeding into the pleural space.

questions.text: A 50-year-old man with a history of alcohol abuse presents with hematemesis. Endoscopy shows esophageal varices. Which vein carries portal blood to the azygos system?
questions.explanation: The left gastric (coronary) vein drains the portal system and anastomoses with esophageal veins (azygos system). Portal hypertension leads to esophageal varices at this site.

questions.text: A 20-year-old man with a fractured mandible has numbness of the lower lip and chin. Which nerve is injured?
questions.explanation: The inferior alveolar nerve (branch of mandibular V3) runs through the mandibular canal. Fracture can injure it; the mental nerve is its terminal branch supplying the lower lip and chin.

questions.text: A patient with a large left-sided pneumothorax has a chest tube inserted at the 5th intercostal space, anterior axillary line. Which muscle is first encountered after skin?
questions.explanation: At the anterior axillary line, the serratus anterior is the most superficial muscle overlying the ribs. The chest tube passes through it before reaching the intercostals.

questions.text: A 65-year-old woman with a femoral neck fracture undergoes hip replacement. During the posterior approach, which nerve is most at risk?
questions.explanation: The sciatic nerve lies posterior to the hip joint and is vulnerable during posterior hip approaches. Retractor placement can injure it, causing foot drop or leg weakness.

questions.text: A 40-year-old man with a stab wound to the epigastrium has a CT showing a pancreatic injury. Which part of the pancreas is most commonly injured in penetrating trauma?
questions.explanation: The body of the pancreas is the most anterior and least protected part, lying directly posterior to the stomach. It is most commonly injured in penetrating epigastric trauma.

questions.text: A patient with a right-sided stroke has difficulty swallowing (dysphagia). Which cranial nerve is most likely affected?
questions.explanation: The vagus nerve (CN X) provides motor innervation to the pharyngeal constrictors and laryngeal muscles. Lesions cause dysphagia and dysphonia.

questions.text: A 55-year-old man with a history of smoking presents with a right apical lung mass and right-sided Horner’s syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis). Which structure is invaded?
questions.explanation: The stellate ganglion (cervicothoracic sympathetic ganglion) lies at the apex of the lung. A Pancoast tumor invades it, causing ipsilateral Horner’s syndrome.

questions.text: A 30-year-old woman with a deep cut to the volar forearm, 2 cm proximal to the wrist, has loss of thumb opposition. Which nerve is cut?
questions.explanation: The recurrent (thenar) branch of the median nerve supplies the thenar muscles (opponens pollicis, abductor pollicis brevis). Injury at the wrist causes loss of thumb opposition.

questions.text: A patient with a large hydrocele undergoes aspiration. The needle passes through the scrotal skin. Which layer is the last before entering the tunica vaginalis?
questions.explanation: The scrotal layers from superficial to deep: skin, dartos, external spermatic fascia, cremasteric fascia, internal spermatic fascia, then tunica vaginalis. Internal spermatic fascia is the last fascial layer.

questions.text: A 60-year-old man with a left ventricular aneurysm develops acute mitral regurgitation. Which papillary muscle is most commonly ruptured after an inferior wall myocardial infarction?
questions.explanation: The posteromedial papillary muscle has a single blood supply (posterior descending artery, usually from RCA). Inferior MI can cause its rupture, leading to acute mitral regurgitation.

questions.text: A 45-year-old woman with a thyroid mass undergoes a total thyroidectomy. Postoperatively, she has a weak, breathy voice. Which nerve is most likely injured?
questions.explanation: Unilateral recurrent laryngeal nerve injury causes ipsilateral vocal cord paralysis, resulting in hoarseness and a weak, breathy voice. This is a common complication of thyroid surgery.

questions.text: A patient with a stab wound to the left lower quadrant presents with a pulsatile mass in the left groin. Which artery is injured?
questions.explanation: The external iliac artery passes along the pelvic brim. A stab wound to the lower quadrant can injure it, leading to a pseudoaneurysm presenting as a pulsatile groin mass.

questions.text: A 70-year-old man with a known abdominal aortic aneurysm presents with sudden-onset testicular pain. Which nerve carries referred pain from the aorta?
questions.explanation: Visceral afferent fibers from the aorta travel with sympathetic nerves that follow the testicular vessels, causing referred pain to the testicle (a classic sign of leaking AAA).

questions.text: A 35-year-old woman with a deep cut to the palmar aspect of the hand at the base of the thumb has loss of sensation over the palmar aspect of the index finger. Which nerve is injured?
questions.explanation: The median nerve provides sensation to the palmar aspect of the lateral 3½ fingers (including the index finger). Injury at the wrist causes sensory loss in that distribution.

questions.text: A patient with a central line placed in the left subclavian vein develops a chylothorax. Which duct is lacerated?
questions.explanation: The thoracic duct empties into the left subclavian vein. Puncture during left subclavian cannulation can lacerate it, causing chylothorax (milky pleural effusion).

questions.text: A 50-year-old man with a right-sided pneumothorax has a chest tube inserted at the 4th intercostal space, midaxillary line. Which nerve runs vertically on the serratus anterior and is at risk?
questions.explanation: The long thoracic nerve (C5–C7) runs vertically on the surface of the serratus anterior muscle. A chest tube inserted too far laterally can injure it, causing winged scapula.

questions.text: A 60-year-old woman with a large goiter presents with stridor. Which structure is compressed causing airway obstruction?
questions.explanation: A large goiter can compress the trachea, causing stridor (noisy breathing) and respiratory distress. This is a surgical emergency.

questions.text: A 25-year-old man with a fractured pelvis after a motor vehicle accident cannot flex his hip. Which nerve is most likely injured?
questions.explanation: The femoral nerve (L2–L4) supplies the iliopsoas (hip flexor) and quadriceps. Pelvic fractures can injure it, causing loss of hip flexion and knee extension.

questions.text: A patient with a stab wound to the right 10th intercostal space, posterior axillary line, has a CT showing a liver laceration. Which ligament attaches the liver to the diaphragm and is torn?
questions.explanation: The coronary ligament attaches the liver to the diaphragm. A penetrating injury at the 10th ICS posteriorly is near the bare area of the liver, tearing the coronary ligament.

questions.text: A 40-year-old man with chronic pancreatitis develops a pseudocyst. The pseudocyst is located in the lesser sac. Which structure forms the anterior wall of the lesser sac?
questions.explanation: The lesser omentum (hepatogastric and hepatoduodenal ligaments) forms the anterior boundary of the lesser sac (omental bursa). The stomach is more anterior.

questions.text: A 55-year-old man with a right-sided lung cancer undergoes a lobectomy. During surgery, the surgeon must identify the right recurrent laryngeal nerve. Where does it loop?
questions.explanation: The right recurrent laryngeal nerve branches from the vagus and loops under the right subclavian artery. The left recurrent laryngeal loops under the aortic arch.

questions.text: A 30-year-old woman with a stab wound to the left posterior triangle of the neck presents with a winged scapula (medial winging). Which nerve is injured?
questions.explanation: The long thoracic nerve runs on the serratus anterior. Injury causes medial winging of the scapula (the medial border lifts off the chest wall when pushing against a wall).

questions.text: A 65-year-old man with a ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm undergoes emergency repair. The surgeon cross-clamps the aorta above the renal arteries. Which collateral pathway maintains lower limb perfusion?
options: Superior mesenteric to inferior mesenteric (Riolan’s arch)
questions.explanation: The marginal artery of Drummond (or Riolan’s arch) connects the superior mesenteric and inferior mesenteric arteries, providing collateral flow to the lower limbs when the aorta is clamped above the renals.

questions.text: A 45-year-old man with a pancreatic head tumor presents with painless jaundice, a palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier’s sign), and weight loss. The tumor is compressing which structure?
questions.explanation: The common bile duct passes through the head of the pancreas. A pancreatic head tumor compresses the CBD, causing obstructive jaundice. The gallbladder becomes palpable due to distention.

questions.text: A 50-year-old woman with a large right ovarian cyst undergoes oophorectomy. During surgery, the ureter is identified crossing which artery at the pelvic brim?
questions.explanation: The ureter crosses the external iliac artery at the pelvic brim, just medial to the ovarian vessels. This is a key landmark to avoid ureteral injury during pelvic surgery.

questions.text: A patient with a stab wound to the left 5th intercostal space, midclavicular line, develops a hemopericardium. Which cardiac chamber is most likely injured?
questions.explanation: The right ventricle forms the anterior cardiac surface and lies behind the 4th–5th intercostal spaces. A stab at the 5th ICS midclavicular line penetrates the right ventricle.

questions.text: A 60-year-old man with a history of smoking presents with a Pancoast tumor. Which structures are involved in Pancoast syndrome?
options: Lower trunk of brachial plexus (C8–T1) and stellate ganglion
Upper trunk of brachial plexus (C5–C6) and phrenic nerve
Middle trunk and recurrent laryngeal nerve
questions.explanation: Pancoast syndrome includes shoulder pain (C8–T1), hand weakness (lower trunk), and Horner’s (stellate ganglion). The subclavian artery may be involved but is not part of the classic triad.

questions.text: A 35-year-old man with a deep cut to the volar forearm, 8 cm proximal to the wrist, has loss of flexion of the distal interphalangeal joint of the index finger. Which nerve is injured?
questions.explanation: The anterior interosseous nerve (branch of median) supplies flexor digitorum profundus to index and middle fingers. Injury causes loss of index DIP flexion (positive \"OK\" sign).

questions.text: A patient with a large left-sided pleural effusion undergoes thoracentesis. The needle is inserted at the 8th intercostal space, posterior axillary line, just above the 8th rib. Which structure is immediately deep?
questions.explanation: At the 8th ICS posterior axillary line, the lung extends to the 10th rib posteriorly. The needle enters the pleural space and lung, not the diaphragm (which is lower posteriorly).

questions.text: A 70-year-old man with a large inguinal hernia undergoes repair. The hernia sac is found medial to the inferior epigastric vessels. This is a direct hernia. Which triangle is defined by these vessels?
questions.explanation: Hesselbach’s triangle is bounded by the inferior epigastric vessels (laterally), rectus abdominis (medially), and inguinal ligament (inferiorly). Direct hernias occur within this triangle.

questions.text: A 40-year-old woman with a central line in the right internal jugular vein develops a right-sided Horner’s syndrome. Which structure was injured?
questions.explanation: The stellate ganglion (cervicothoracic sympathetic ganglion) lies on the longus colli muscle at C7. A deep IJ puncture can injure it, causing ipsilateral Horner’s syndrome.

questions.text: A 55-year-old man with a retroperitoneal sarcoma undergoes resection. The tumor is located anterior to the psoas muscle, medial to the ureter, and lateral to the aorta. Which lymph node group is involved?
questions.explanation: Para-aortic lymph nodes lie along the abdominal aorta, anterior to the psoas, medial to ureters. This location is classic for retroperitoneal sarcomas or lymphoma.

questions.text: A patient with a stab wound to the left upper quadrant develops a splenic injury. Which ligament contains the short gastric vessels that may be torn during splenectomy?
questions.explanation: The gastrosplenic ligament connects the stomach to the spleen and contains the short gastric arteries. During splenectomy, this ligament is ligated to avoid gastric ischemia.

questions.text: A 30-year-old man with a gunshot wound to the left lower quadrant presents with a pulsatile mass in the left groin. Which artery is injured?
questions.explanation: A gunshot to the lower quadrant can injure the external iliac artery as it passes along the pelvic brim. A pseudoaneurysm may present as a pulsatile groin mass.

questions.text: A patient with chronic liver disease develops caput medusae. Which vein carries portal blood to the systemic circulation through the paraumbilical veins?
options: Left portal vein → umbilical vein → epigastric veins
Superior mesenteric vein → inferior vena cava
questions.explanation: The left portal vein connects to the paraumbilical veins, which anastomose with epigastric veins of the anterior abdominal wall. Portal hypertension shunts blood here → caput medusae.

questions.text: A 50-year-old man with a large left-sided hydrocele undergoes aspiration. The needle is inserted through the scrotal skin. Which layer is the first encountered after the dartos fascia?
questions.explanation: The scrotal layers from superficial to deep: skin, dartos fascia, external spermatic fascia (from external oblique aponeurosis), cremasteric fascia, internal spermatic fascia, then tunica vaginalis.

questions.text: A patient with a thyroid mass undergoes a total thyroidectomy. Postoperatively, she has a weak, breathy voice and difficulty swallowing. Which nerve is likely injured?
questions.explanation: The external branch of the superior laryngeal nerve supplies the cricothyroid muscle, which tenses the vocal cord. Injury causes loss of high pitch, voice fatigue, and mild hoarseness.

questions.text: A 60-year-old man with a history of COPD presents with a spontaneous pneumothorax. A chest tube is placed in the 4th intercostal space, anterior axillary line. Which vessel runs in the subcostal groove of the 4th rib and is at risk?
questions.explanation: The intercostal artery, vein, and nerve run in the subcostal groove of each rib. A chest tube placed too low (below the rib) can injure the neurovascular bundle.

questions.text: A 35-year-old woman with a stab wound to the right upper quadrant at the 6th intercostal space, anterior axillary line, has a liver laceration. Which segment of the liver is most likely injured?
questions.explanation: The right lobe anterior inferior segment (Segment V) lies just deep to the 6th–7th intercostal spaces anteriorly. A stab here commonly injures Segment V or VI.

questions.text: A patient with a central line placed in the right internal jugular vein develops a hemothorax. The needle likely injured which artery?
questions.explanation: The subclavian artery lies posterior to the clavicle and medial to the internal jugular vein. A low IJ approach angled too laterally can puncture the subclavian artery, bleeding into the pleural space.

questions.text: A 45-year-old man with a gunshot wound to the epigastrium has a CT showing injury to the celiac trunk. Which organs would be most immediately ischemic?
questions.explanation: The celiac trunk supplies the foregut: liver (via hepatic artery), spleen (via splenic artery), and stomach (via left gastric artery). The pancreas and duodenum receive additional supply from SMA.

questions.text: A 70-year-old woman with a large hiatal hernia presents with chest pain and dysphagia. Which anatomical hiatus is involved, and what structure passes through it?
options: Aortic hiatus – aorta and thoracic duct
Esophageal hiatus – esophagus and vagus nerves
Caval hiatus – IVC and phrenic nerve
Foramen of Morgagni – internal thoracic vessels
Foramen of Bochdalek – no normal structures
questions.explanation: The esophageal hiatus is in the right crus of the diaphragm at T10, passing esophagus and anterior/posterior vagal trunks. A hiatal hernia occurs when stomach herniates through this hiatus.

questions.text: A patient with a pancreatic pseudocyst presents with sudden-onset left upper quadrant pain and a palpable mass. The pseudocyst has ruptured into which space?
questions.explanation: Pancreatic pseudocysts typically form in the lesser sac (omental bursa), which lies posterior to the stomach. Rupture often remains localized but can extend into the greater sac.

questions.text: A 25-year-old man with a stab wound to the left anterior chest at the 2nd intercostal space, parasternal region, develops a large left pneumothorax. Which anatomical space is entered?
questions.explanation: The 2nd intercostal space parasternal region overlies the left upper lobe and pleural cavity. Air enters the pleural space, causing pneumothorax. The heart is deeper and more medial.

questions.text: A 60-year-old man with a pseudoaneurysm of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA). Which structure passes anterior to the SMA at its origin from the aorta?
questions.explanation: The uncinate process of the pancreas lies anterior to the SMA origin. The left renal vein passes between aorta and SMA (posterior to SMA). The third part of duodenum passes posterior to SMA.

questions.text: A patient with a large retroperitoneal sarcoma arising from the psoas muscle presents with femoral nerve palsy. The femoral nerve emerges from which lumbar roots and passes between which two muscles?
options: L1–L3 between psoas and quadratus lumborum
L2–L4 between psoas and transversus abdominis
questions.explanation: The femoral nerve (L2–L4) emerges from the lateral border of psoas major, then runs in the groove between psoas and iliacus, under the inguinal ligament.

questions.text: A 45-year-old woman undergoes a right hemicolectomy for colon cancer. During the surgery, the surgeon identifies the ureter crossing which structure at the pelvic brim?
questions.explanation: The ureter crosses the external iliac artery at the pelvic brim, medial to the gonadal vessels. This is a critical landmark to avoid ureteral injury during pelvic or colorectal surgery.

questions.text: A 30-year-old man with a stab wound to the left lateral chest at the 8th intercostal space, midaxillary line, has a CT showing a diaphragmatic injury. Which structure forms the posterior attachment of the diaphragm at this level?
questions.explanation: The diaphragm has a lumbar origin from the crura (right crus from L1–L3, left from L1–L2). Posteriorly, it attaches to the lumbar vertebrae via crura. The 8th ICS posteriorly is near the lumbar origin.

questions.text: A 55-year-old man with a history of myocardial infarction presents with a left ventricular aneurysm. On auscultation, a murmur is heard. Which papillary muscle is most commonly ruptured after an inferior wall MI?
questions.explanation: The posteromedial papillary muscle has a single blood supply from the posterior descending artery (usually right coronary). Inferior MI can rupture it, causing acute mitral regurgitation.

questions.text: A patient with a central venous catheter placed in the left subclavian vein develops a chylothorax. Which structure is most likely lacerated?
questions.explanation: The thoracic duct ascends on the left side of the mediastinum, crossing from right to left at T5–T6. A left subclavian vein puncture can injure the thoracic duct, causing chylothorax.

questions.text: A 40-year-old man with a stab wound to the right flank at the 12th rib angle has a CT showing a perinephric hematoma. Which part of the kidney is most vulnerable?
questions.explanation: The lower pole of the kidney is less protected by ribs (the 12th rib ends at the upper pole). A stab below the 12th rib easily injures the lower pole and perinephric space.

questions.text: A 50-year-old man with chronic pancreatitis develops a pseudocyst in the lesser sac. Which structure forms the anterior wall of the lesser sac?
questions.explanation: The lesser omentum (hepatogastric and hepatoduodenal ligaments) forms the anterior boundary of the lesser sac (omental bursa). The stomach lies more anteriorly but is not the wall itself.

questions.text: A 35-year-old woman with a stab wound to the right lower quadrant at McBurney’s point has a perforated appendix. Which structure is immediately deep to the parietal peritoneum at this point?
questions.explanation: McBurney’s point overlies the base of the appendix. A stab at this point can directly perforate the appendix, causing peritonitis.

questions.text: A 60-year-old man with a right-sided kidney stone undergoes lithotripsy. The stone is in the renal pelvis. Which anatomical landmark on the posterior abdominal wall is used to target the stone?
questions.explanation: The costovertebral angle (angle between 12th rib and vertebral column) overlies the kidney. Lithotripsy focuses shock waves through this area.

questions.text: A 25-year-old man with a stab wound to the anterior neck at the level of the thyroid cartilage presents with massive subcutaneous emphysema extending to the chest. Which anatomical space allows air to spread from the neck to the mediastinum?
questions.explanation: The danger space (between alar and prevertebral fascia) extends from skull base to diaphragm. Air from a tracheal or esophageal injury can dissect into the mediastinum via this space.

questions.text: A 65-year-old man with a large abdominal aortic aneurysm undergoes open repair. During the procedure, the surgeon must ligate the lumbar arteries. Which vertebral level corresponds to the origin of the most common lumbar arteries?
questions.explanation: The lumbar arteries (typically four pairs) arise from the posterior aorta at L1–L4 levels. They supply the posterior abdominal wall and spinal cord, and ligation can rarely cause spinal cord ischemia.

questions.text: A patient with a stab wound to the right lower quadrant at McBurney’s point presents with peritonitis. Which structure is immediately deep to the parietal peritoneum at this point?
questions.explanation: McBurney’s point overlies the base of the appendix (normally retrocecal but often pelvic). This is the classic site for tenderness in acute appendicitis; a stab here can perforate the appendix.

questions.text: A 50-year-old man with a right-sided kidney stone undergoes extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy. The stone is located in the renal pelvis. Which anatomical landmark on the posterior abdominal wall is used to target the stone?
questions.explanation: The costovertebral angle (angle between 12th rib and vertebral column) overlies the kidney. Lithotripsy focuses shock waves through this area to target renal pelvic stones.

questions.text: A 25-year-old man with a stab wound to the anterior neck at the level of the thyroid cartilage presents with a massive subcutaneous emphysema extending from the neck to the chest. Which anatomical space allows air to spread from the neck to the mediastinum?
questions.explanation: The danger space (between alar and prevertebral fascia) extends from the skull base to the diaphragm. Air from a tracheal or esophageal injury can dissect into the mediastinum via this space.

questions.text: A patient with a large pleural effusion undergoes thoracentesis. The needle is inserted just above the 9th rib in the midaxillary line. Which anatomical principle prevents injury to the neurovascular bundle?
options: The bundle runs in the subcostal groove, below the rib
The bundle runs above the rib
The bundle is protected by endothoracic fascia
questions.explanation: The intercostal neurovascular bundle runs in the subcostal groove of the upper rib (i.e., below each rib). Needle insertion just above the lower rib (e.g., above the 9th rib) avoids the bundle of the 9th rib.

questions.text: A 70-year-old woman with a pathological fracture of the femoral neck undergoes a hemiarthroplasty. During the procedure, the sciatic nerve is at risk. Which landmark is used to identify the sciatic nerve posteriorly?
questions.explanation: The sciatic nerve emerges from the greater sciatic foramen and runs midway between the greater trochanter (lateral) and ischial tuberosity (medial). This landmark avoids injury during posterior hip approaches.

questions.text: A 45-year-old man with a stab wound to the left anterior chest at the 3rd intercostal space, 2 cm left of the sternum, presents with a pericardial effusion. Which structure lies immediately deep to this site?
questions.explanation: The right ventricle forms the anterior cardiac surface. The 3rd ICS left parasternal area overlies the right ventricular outflow tract, which is vulnerable to penetrating injury.

questions.text: A patient with a thyroid nodule undergoes a fine-needle aspiration. The needle passes through the platysma and the investing layer of deep cervical fascia. Which muscle lies immediately deep to the investing fascia in the anterior midline?
questions.explanation: The investing fascia covers the sternocleidomastoid and trapezius, but in the anterior midline, the next layer is the infrahyoid strap muscles, the most superficial being sternohyoid.

questions.text: A 60-year-old man with a history of smoking presents with a left upper lobe mass and new-onset left arm swelling. Which vein is compressed by the mass at the thoracic inlet?
questions.explanation: The left subclavian vein passes over the first rib and under the clavicle. A left apical lung tumor can compress it, causing unilateral arm swelling (Paget-von Schrötter syndrome).

questions.text: A 30-year-old woman with a stab wound to the right lower quadrant presents with an injury to the right ureter. The ureter is most likely injured at which of the following anatomical narrowings?
options: Crossing of the pelvic brim (over iliac vessels)
questions.explanation: The ureter has three narrowings: UPJ, pelvic brim (over iliac vessels), and intramural. The pelvic brim narrowing is most vulnerable to penetrating trauma and iatrogenic injury during pelvic surgery.

questions.text: A patient with a large goiter undergoes thyroidectomy. Postoperatively, he develops a hoarse voice and stridor. Which nerve injury is most likely?
questions.explanation: Bilateral recurrent laryngeal nerve injury causes both vocal cords to assume a paramedian position, leading to airway obstruction (stridor) and aphonia. Unilateral injury causes hoarseness without stridor.

questions.text: A 55-year-old man with a ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm undergoes emergency repair. The surgeon cross-clamps the aorta just below the renal arteries. Which collateral pathway can maintain spinal cord perfusion?
options: Artery of Adamkiewicz from a lumbar artery
questions.explanation: The artery of Adamkiewicz (great radicular artery) arises from a left posterior intercostal or lumbar artery (T9–L2) and supplies the lower two-thirds of the spinal cord. It is a critical collateral after aortic clamping.

questions.text: A 40-year-old woman with a central line placed in the right internal jugular vein develops a right-sided Horner’s syndrome. The needle likely injured which structure?
questions.explanation: The stellate ganglion (cervicothoracic sympathetic ganglion) lies on the longus colli muscle at the C7 level, medial to the subclavian artery. A deep IJ puncture can injure it, causing ipsilateral Horner’s.

questions.text: A 25-year-old man with a stab wound to the left posterior triangle of the neck presents with a winged scapula. Which nerve is injured?
questions.explanation: The long thoracic nerve runs superficially on the serratus anterior muscle. It is vulnerable in the posterior triangle as it passes through the middle scalene. Injury causes medial winging of the scapula.

questions.text: A 60-year-old man with a pancreatic head tumor undergoes a Whipple procedure. During the surgery, the surgeon must ligate the gastroduodenal artery. Which artery does the gastroduodenal artery arise from?
questions.explanation: The gastroduodenal artery is the first branch of the common hepatic artery (which is a branch of the celiac trunk). It passes posterior to the first part of the duodenum.

questions.text: A patient with a massive lower gastrointestinal bleed undergoes a mesenteric angiogram. The bleeding is from the sigmoid colon. Which artery is the source?
questions.explanation: The sigmoid colon is supplied by the inferior mesenteric artery via its sigmoid branches. The superior mesenteric supplies the small bowel and right colon.

questions.text: A 70-year-old man with a large left inguinal hernia undergoes repair. During surgery, the surgeon identifies the hernia sac medial to the inferior epigastric vessels. This is a direct inguinal hernia. Which anatomical triangle is defined by these vessels?
questions.explanation: Hesselbach’s triangle is bounded by the inferior epigastric vessels (laterally), rectus abdominis (medially), and inguinal ligament (inferiorly). Direct hernias occur within this triangle, medial to the vessels.

questions.text: A patient with a stab wound to the right 5th intercostal space at the midclavicular line develops a right-sided hemothorax. Which intercostal artery is most likely injured?
questions.explanation: The 5th intercostal artery runs in the subcostal groove of the 5th rib. A stab at the 5th ICS will injure the artery of that space. The internal thoracic artery is more medial.

questions.text: A 35-year-old woman with a deep cut to the volar forearm, 5 cm proximal to the wrist, presents with an inability to flex the index finger’s distal interphalangeal joint. Which nerve is injured?
questions.explanation: The anterior interosseous nerve (branch of median) supplies the flexor digitorum profundus to the index and middle fingers (and flexor pollicis longus). Injury causes loss of index DIP flexion (positive “OK” sign).

questions.text: A 50-year-old man with a large right-sided pleural effusion undergoes thoracentesis. The needle is inserted in the 8th intercostal space, posterior axillary line, just above the 8th rib. Which structure is immediately deep to the needle tip at this site?
questions.explanation: At the 8th ICS posterior axillary line, the needle is still above the diaphragmatic reflection (which is lower posteriorly). The lung extends to the 10th rib posteriorly, so the needle enters pleural space and lung.

questions.text: A patient with a massive upper GI bleed from esophageal varices undergoes a transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS). The catheter passes from the hepatic vein into the portal vein through which part of the liver?
options: Right hepatic vein to right portal vein
Middle hepatic vein to left portal vein
Left hepatic vein to main portal vein
Inferior vena cava to umbilical vein
questions.explanation: TIPS creates a communication between the right hepatic vein (or middle) and the intrahepatic portion of the right portal vein, usually passing through the liver parenchyma.

questions.text: A 60-year-old man with a history of smoking presents with a Pancoast tumor. Which structures are involved in Pancoast syndrome?
options: C8–T1 nerve roots, stellate ganglion, and subclavian artery
Phrenic nerve, vagus, and recurrent laryngeal
Brachial plexus upper trunk and subclavian vein
Lower trunk of brachial plexus and stellate ganglion
questions.explanation: Pancoast syndrome includes shoulder pain (C8–T1 involvement), Horner’s (stellate ganglion), and hand weakness (lower trunk). The subclavian artery may also be involved but is not part of the classic triad.

questions.text: A 45-year-old man with a stab wound to the left upper quadrant presents with a splenic laceration. The surgeon performs a splenectomy. Which ligament must be divided to mobilize the spleen from the left kidney?
questions.explanation: The splenorenal (lienorenal) ligament connects the spleen to the left kidney and contains the splenic artery and vein. Dividing it allows medial mobilization of the spleen.

questions.text: A 55-year-old man with a left-sided diaphragmatic hernia from a previous stab wound presents with bowel sounds in the chest. Which part of the diaphragm is most commonly affected in acquired hernias?
questions.explanation: Acquired traumatic hernias are most common on the left posterolateral aspect (Bochdalek’s foramen is congenital but traumatic hernias occur in the weaker left posterior area).

questions.text: A 60-year-old woman with ovarian cancer develops malignant ascites. Paracentesis is performed in the left lower quadrant. Which abdominal wall layer is the last barrier before the peritoneum?
questions.explanation: The abdominal wall layers are: skin, subcutaneous fat, external oblique, internal oblique, transversus abdominis, transversalis fascia, extraperitoneal fat, then parietal peritoneum. Transversalis fascia is the last fascial layer.

questions.text: A 35-year-old man with a stab wound to the right flank at the 12th rib angle has a CT showing a perinephric hematoma. Which kidney structure is most vulnerable in this location?
questions.explanation: The lower pole of the kidney is less protected by ribs (the 12th rib ends at the upper pole). A stab below the 12th rib easily injures the lower pole and perinephric space.

questions.text: A 50-year-old man with chronic pancreatitis develops a pseudocyst in the lesser sac. Which structure forms the anterior wall of the lesser sac?
questions.explanation: The lesser omentum (hepatogastric and hepatoduodenal ligaments) forms the anterior boundary of the lesser sac (omental bursa). The stomach lies more anteriorly but is not the wall itself.

questions.text: A 70-year-old man with benign prostatic hyperplasia undergoes transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP). During the procedure, the capsule is perforated, and irrigating fluid extravasates. Which space is most likely filled?
questions.explanation: The retropubic space (space of Retzius) lies between the pubic symphysis and the bladder/prostate. Perforation of the prostatic capsule allows fluid into this extraperitoneal space.

questions.text: A patient with a large left atrial myxoma presents with systemic embolization to the left leg. The embolus traveled from the left atrium to the left ventricle, then through which valve?
questions.explanation: From left ventricle, blood passes through the aortic valve into the aorta. A left atrial myxoma fragment embolizes to systemic circulation via left ventricle → aortic valve → aorta.

questions.text: A 25-year-old man with a deep cut to the palmar aspect of the wrist at the level of the distal skin crease presents with loss of thumb opposition and thenar atrophy. Which nerve is transected?
questions.explanation: The median nerve lies just deep to the palmaris longus tendon at the wrist. Division at this level paralyzes the thenar muscles (recurrent branch), causing loss of thumb opposition.
